alloy_matches.reason: Same nominal composition: Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al, same base element and alloying elements.

process_matches.reason: Same processing steps: homogenization at 1100°C, hot rolling above 1000°C, cold rolling to 1.5mm, intercritical annealing at 850°C for 10min, water quench.

sample_matches.reason: Both represent sample along rolling direction (RD) with mechanical properties.
Both represent sample along transverse direction (TD) with mechanical properties.

truth.papers.A29.title: On the anisotropic deformation behavior of a low-density medium manganese steel
truth.papers.A29.paper_scope: Investigate anisotropic deformation behavior of a banded medium-Mn lightweight steel containing δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained γ+α aggregates, with focus on strain partitioning and martensitic transformation under different loading directions.

prediction.papers.A29.title: On the anisotropic deformation behavior of a low-density medium manganese steel
prediction.papers.A29.paper_scope: Investigation of anisotropic deformation behavior in a banded low-density medium-Mn lightweight steel under rolling (RD) and transverse (TD) directions using in-situ synchrotron HEXRD and high-resolution microscopic digital image correlation.

truth.alloys.alloy_8mn5al0_3c.alloys_notes: Mass density calculated to be ~7.40 g/cm³; Al addition reduces density by 6.2%.

prediction.alloys.alloy_8mn5al0_3c.alloys_notes: Calculated mass density ~7.40 g/cm3; Al addition reduces density by 6.2% and promotes δ-ferrite formation.

truth.processes.proc_ia850_10min.description: Ingot homogenized at 1100°C, hot-rolled to ~3 mm (temperature maintained above ~1000°C), cold-rolled to ~1.5 mm, then intercritically annealed at 850°C for 10 min, followed by water quenching.
truth.processes.proc_ia850_10min.processes_not: Intercritical annealing promotes formation of ultrafine γ+α aggregate and δ-ferrite banding.

prediction.processes.proc_ia850_10min.description: Vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1100°C, hot-rolling above ~1000°C to ~3 mm, cold-rolling to ~1.5 mm, intercritical annealing at 850°C for 10 min followed by water quenching to room temperature.

prediction.processing_steps.sample_rd.processing_st: Water quenching to room temperature to obtain ultrafine γ+α aggregate and δ-ferrite.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_td.processing_st: Water quenching to room temperature to obtain ultrafine γ+α aggregate and δ-ferrite.

truth.structures.sample_undeformed.overall_str: Banded microstructure consisting of coarse-grained δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained γ+α aggregates aligned along RD.
truth.structures.sample_undeformed.microstruct: δ-ferrite: 25 vol.%; α-ferrite: 44 vol.%; austenite: 31 vol.%; γ+α aggregate fraction: 56 vol.%
Coarse-grained, banded along RD; grain size ~38 μm

prediction.structures.sample_rd.overall_structure: Banded or layered structure along rolling direction consisting of coarse δ-ferrite bands and ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α aggregates.
prediction.structures.sample_rd.microstructure_list: Bimodal: UFG γ+α aggregates (~1.8–2 μm) and coarse δ-ferrite (~38 μm)
δ-ferrite exhibits a rolling texture with major rotated cube component {100}<011>
prediction.structures.sample_td.overall_structure: Deformed microstructure at 15% engineering strain showing strain-induced α'-martensite nucleated primarily at phase boundaries.
prediction.structures.sample_td.microstructure_list: UFG with stacking faults and planar slip
Bimodal: UFG γ+α aggregates (~1.8–2 μm) and coarse δ-ferrite (~38 μm)
δ-ferrite exhibits a rolling texture with major rotated cube component {100}<011>
Nucleated at γ/α and γ/δ phase boundaries
High GND density at heterointerfaces due to strain incompatibility

truth.interfaces.sample_undeformed.phases[aust: semi-coherent; high GND density at boundaries
truth.interfaces.sample_undeformed.defect_inte: Strain incompatibility leads to accumulation of geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs) at phase boundaries, providing nucleation sites for α'-martensite
KAM and μ-DIC show high local misorientation and strain at γ/α and γ/δ interfaces
truth.interfaces.sample_undeformed.interface_n: Under tensile loading, strain concentrates at phase boundaries; in TD sample, higher strain in δ-ferrite; in RD sample, more uniform strain between δ and γ+α.
Dislocation planar slip and stacking faults in austenite; cross-slip and tangles in ferrite phases.
truth.interfaces.sample_undeformed.phase_evolu: Strain-induced martensitic transformation from γ to α'-martensite, nucleating preferentially at γ/α and γ/δ boundaries.

prediction.interfaces.sample_rd.defect_interaction[: High density of geometrically necessary dislocations due to strain incompatibility between austenite and ferrite during deformation
Strain concentrations at δ-ferrite/UFG γ+α phase boundaries observed by μ-DIC and KAM maps under tensile loading
prediction.interfaces.sample_rd.phase_evolution: Strain-induced α'-martensite nucleates primarily at austenite/ferrite (α or δ) phase boundaries due to high stress/strain concentration and interface defects such as ledges.
prediction.interfaces.sample_td.defect_interaction[: High density of geometrically necessary dislocations due to strain incompatibility between austenite and ferrite during deformation
Strain concentrations at δ-ferrite/UFG γ+α phase boundaries observed by μ-DIC and KAM maps under tensile loading
prediction.interfaces.sample_td.phase_evolution: Strain-induced α'-martensite nucleates primarily at austenite/ferrite (α or δ) phase boundaries due to high stress/strain concentration and interface defects such as ledges.

prediction.properties.sample_rd.mechanical.tensile_: Engineering stress-strain curve shows higher strength and ductility than TD sample
prediction.properties.sample_td.mechanical.tensile_: Engineering stress-strain curve shows lower strength and ductility than RD sample

truth.characterization_methods.sample_td.key_f: TD: pronounced strain partitioning; higher strain in δ-ferrite (1.26× that of γ+α); strain also concentrates in γ within UFG region.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_rd.key_f: RD: uniform strain between δ-ferrite and γ+α; more strain in γ within UFG region.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_rd.key_f: UFG γ+α region: 221±3.0 HV; δ-ferrite region: 205±1.5 HV.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_td.purpo: Investigate strain partitioning between δ-ferrite and UFG γ+α aggregates during tensile deformation
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_td.key_f: Pronounced strain heterogeneity with higher strain concentrated in δ-ferrite (iso-stress-like condition); within UFG region, strain concentrated in γ-α' mixed phase (~1.3× higher than α-ferrite).

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Fitted curves compared with HEXRD experimental martensite fraction data for both RD and TD samples (Fig. 14); the model accurately predicts martensite kinetics when accounting for direction-dependent strain partitioning.
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Predict deformation-induced martensite volume fraction as a function of plastic strain
α=1.35, β=2, n=2, initial austenite fraction F_γ0=46.5 vol.%; for RD sample, austenite plastic strain taken as ε_RD = 1.15 ε_TD based on micro-DIC data
Assumes random martensite nucleation with n=2; parameters obtained from TD sample fitting then applied to RD sample with adjusted austenite strain.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The overall tensile strength range of the investigated steel is ~855±35 MPa with total elongation 65±5% (room temperature).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: RD sample higher strain hardening ability attributed to more martensite formation due to higher strain in austenite under iso-strain condition.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: OC model (α=1.35, β=2, n=2) successfully describes martensite fraction evolution; RD data matched by considering 1.15× higher plastic strain in austenite compared to TD.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Texture of δ-ferrite is {100}<011> rotated cube with max intensity 2.57; its effect on anisotropic hardening considered insignificant.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Lattice strain measurements show austenite yields first (deviation from linearity at 1.8% strain RD, 2.1% TD) while ferrite yields later (2.3% RD, 2.9% TD).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Strain-induced α'-martensite fraction negligible below 0.03 true strain; at high strains difference between RD and TD martensite fraction up to 4 vol.%.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: In RD sample, δ-ferrite and γ+α aggregates deform in iso-strain condition; in TD sample, iso-stress condition leads to strain concentration in δ-ferrite.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The intercritically annealed steel demonstrates an overall tensile strength of ~855±35 MPa and total elongation of 65±5% (averaged or representative values).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Figure 4(a) provides engineering stress-strain curves showing the RD sample reaches ~864 MPa UTS and 69% elongation, while the TD sample exhibits lower strength and ductility (exact TD peak values not explicitly stated in text).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Figure 8(b) shows deformation-induced martensite fraction versus true strain: the RD sample reaches ~25% martensite at true strain 0.5, while the TD sample reaches ~21%; the difference is as high as ~4 vol.% at true strain ~0.48.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Figure 9(b) shows austenite dislocation density versus true strain: RD sample reaches ~10×10^14 m^-2 at true strain 0.5, while TD reaches ~9.5×10^14 m^-2; the difference diminishes above true strain 0.35.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Figure 12 shows average shear strain partitioning: in the RD sample, δ-ferrite and γ+α aggregates deform similarly (iso-strain condition), whereas in the TD sample the δ-ferrite strain is ~1.26 times higher than γ+α aggregates; within UFG regions, the γ-α' mixed phase strain is up to ~1.3 times higher than α-ferrite for both samples.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Figure 13 shows weighted average lattice strain evolution: austenite yields earlier than ferrite at applied strains of 1.8% (RD) and 2.1% (TD), while ferrite yields at 2.3% (RD) and 2.9% (TD).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Supplementary Fig. S1 indicates the δ-phase has a rotated cube texture {100}<011> with a maximum intensity of 2.57; calculated Taylor factors are similar for RD (2.83) and TD (2.78), suggesting crystallographic texture is not the dominant cause of anisotropy.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: The volume expansion associated with the γ→α' martensitic transformation is approximately 2~4%, contributing to increased local plastic strain in the γ-α' mixed phase.
